title: Julius Weitzdörfer: Managing the Impact of Nuclear Disasters
summary: Julius Weitzdörfer’s earliest childhood memories are the major global events of 1986—the Space Shuttle Challenger explosion and the Chernobyl disaster. Looking back, he realizes that these events must have contributed to shaping his later research interests in managing technological risks.
body: Julius Weitzdörfer is a Stanton Nuclear Security Junior Faculty Fellow The latest from Belfer See more Julius Weitzdörfer’s earliest childhood memories are the major global events of 1986—the Space Shuttle Challenger explosion and the Chernobyl disaster. Looking back, he realizes that these events must have contributed to shaping his later research interests in managing technological risks. Weitzdörfer is a Stanton Nuclear Security Junior Faculty Fellow at the Belfer Center, following four years at the Center for the Study of Existential Risk at Cambridge, England. At the Belfer Center, he studies recovery from nuclear or radiological disasters as a joint fellow with the Project on Managing the Atom and the International Security Program. Weitzdörfer, who spent three years in Japan, including in the wake of the Fukushima nuclear disaster, focuses his research on the law and policy responses of the Japanese government. Among the foundations of risk management is the idea that “every risk has a probability side and an impact side,” he said. “If you want to reduce the risk, of course it’s good to reduce the probability, but you can also make progress by reducing the potential impact of the event.” Triage, evacuation, resettlement, decontamination, and, if possible, returning to the site of a disaster, are all important for rebuilding communities and economies following a nuclear incident. The faster these steps happen, the better for everyone. In Fukushima, every year people have been unable to return has cost the country an additional $10 billion. Weitzdörfer hopes policies can be established to better prepare for nuclear “error and terror” scenarios—wherever they happen. “The atom is quite literally the smallest scale thing for a policymaker to think about... But on the other hand, the consequences of bad management of the atom—nuclear war—would be likely the most existential and largest scale issue a policymaker could think of.” “Future generations will not forgive us,” Weitzdörfer said, “if we do not govern nuclear technologies responsibly and sustainably.” Recommended citation Carey, Brilee. "Julius Weitzdörfer: Managing the Impact of Nuclear Disasters." Belfer Center Newsletter, Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School. (Fall/Winter 2019-2020). _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: Julius Weitzdörfer, a Stanton Nuclear Security Junior Faculty Fellow at the Belfer Center, studies recovery from nuclear or radiological disasters. His research, informed by his time in Japan after Fukushima, focuses on the law and policy responses to such events. He emphasizes that risk management involves reducing both the probability and the impact of disasters. Key recovery steps include triage, evacuation, resettlement, decontamination, and facilitating return, with speed being crucial to minimize economic costs, as seen in Fukushima's annual $10 billion loss from displacement. Weitzdörfer advocates for policies to prepare for nuclear 'error and terror' scenarios, warning that the mismanagement of nuclear technology poses an existential threat, and future generations demand responsible governance.
result.main_content_zh: 朱利叶斯·魏茨德费尔是贝尔弗中心的斯坦顿核安全初级教员研究员，研究核或放射性灾难后的恢复工作。他在日本福岛核事故后的经历为其研究提供了依据，重点关注日本政府的法律和政策应对。他强调风险管理包括降低灾难发生的概率和潜在影响。灾后恢复的关键步骤包括分类救治、疏散、重新安置、去污以及尽可能让居民返回家园，这些步骤的推进速度至关重要，延迟会导致巨大经济损失，例如福岛每年因人员无法返回造成的100亿美元损失。魏茨德费尔主张制定政策，以更好地为核“错误与恐怖”情景做好准备。他指出，核技术管理不善可能引发核战争，这是决策者可能面对的最具生存威胁和最大规模的问题，并警告说如果不对核技术进行负责任和可持续的治理，后代将不会原谅我们。
